result:
The world is loud, but sometimes the most interesting things happen in the quiet corners where nobody’s looking. I’ve been thinking lately about how much of human experience is shaped by the stories we refuse to tell. Not the polished ones we post online or recount at dinner parties, but the messy, half-formed ones that live in the back of our minds like uninvited houseguests who won’t leave.

There’s something strangely beautiful about the way memory works. It’s not a library. It’s more like a drunk uncle telling the same story differently every time. I remember being seven years old and convinced that if I stared at the night sky long enough, I could see the exact moment when a star died. I had no concept of light years or the fact that the light I was seeing had already been traveling longer than my species had existed. I just knew that the universe felt impossibly big and I felt impossibly small, and somehow that made everything okay.

We spend so much of our lives trying to feel less small. We build careers, relationships, social media empires, home gyms, book collections we’ll never finish, skincare routines that promise to reverse time itself. All of it, in some way, a rebellion against the cosmic indifference that first made us stare up at the sky and feel that delicious terror.

I’ve been watching people more than usual lately. Not in a creepy way (though let’s be honest, all observation has a touch of voyeurism). Just noticing how everyone seems to be performing competence while secretly winging it. The confident CEO who still feels like an imposter when she walks into a boardroom. The comedian who makes everyone laugh while quietly wondering if he’s lovable when he’s not performing. The perfectly curated Instagram mother who cries in her car between school drop-offs. We’re all such magnificent pretenders.

And yet, there’s a strange comfort in that universal fraudulence. It means none of us are actually alone in our uncertainty. The person you think has it all figured out is probably lying awake at 3 a.m. wondering if they chose the right path, or if they’re a good enough parent, or if that thing they said in 2009 still haunts them somewhere in someone else’s memory.

I’ve started collecting what I call “human moments.” They’re those tiny instances where the mask slips and you see the real person underneath. Like when the gruff old man at the hardware store suddenly tears up talking about his dog that died last spring. Or the way strangers in airports will lock eyes during turbulence and share a look that says “I’m scared too. Let’s be scared together.” These moments feel like tiny acts of revolution against the isolation of modern life.

Sometimes I wonder what it would be like if we all just admitted how lost we are. What if instead of asking “How are you?” we started asking “What are you pretending not to feel today?” The answers might break us open in the best possible way.

I’ve been trying to get better at being lost. There’s an art to it. The Buddhists have their concept of beginner’s mind, but I prefer the term “professional amateur.” Someone who has mastered the skill of not knowing, of staying curious, of approaching life like it’s a book you’re reading for the first time even though you’ve been turning its pages for decades.

The trees outside my window right now are doing their annual quiet miracle. They don’t announce it. They don’t post about it. They just turn green, then gold, then bare, in perfect rhythm with something ancient and wordless. I find this deeply comforting. The natural world doesn’t seem to suffer from the same existential FOMO that plagues the rest of us. A tree doesn’t worry about whether it’s doing enough with its life. It just grows. Loses leaves. Grows again. Trusts the cycle.

Maybe that’s the real flex - trusting the cycle. Believing that even when everything looks like death (bare branches, empty nests, long winters of the soul), something is still working beneath the surface. Roots are digging deeper. Energy is being stored. The comeback is being prepared in darkness.

I’ve had my own dark seasons. Times when it felt like the sun had filed for divorce and taken all the warmth with it. But looking back, those were also the seasons when I learned the most. Not in the tidy, inspirational-quote way. The learning was messy and slow and often happened while I was crying in the shower or eating cereal for dinner or staring at walls. Growth doesn’t always announce itself with trumpets. Sometimes it sounds like silence. Sometimes it feels like nothing at all until one day you realize you can breathe a little easier than you could six months ago.

I’ve been thinking about love lately too. Not the fireworks version, but the quiet, stubborn kind that shows up even when it’s inconvenient. The kind that stays after the dopamine has worn off and the real work begins. Love as a daily practice of choosing to see someone clearly (their flaws, their fears, their ridiculous way of loading the dishwasher) and still saying yes to all of it.

We’re terrible at talking about this kind of love because it’s not particularly Instagrammable. It doesn’t come with soundtracks or perfect lighting. It looks like someone holding your hair while you vomit at 2 a.m. It looks like remembering how they take their coffee even when you’re fighting. It looks like choosing to fight for someone instead of with them.

The older I get, the more I’m convinced that the point of all this (the joy, the grief, the boredom, the wonder, the Tuesday afternoons that feel like nothing and everything) is simply to become more human. Not better. Not more successful. Not even happier, necessarily. Just more human. More porous. More capable of holding the contradictions. More willing to be moved by things. More open to being wrong. More in love with the mystery of existing at all.

Because let’s be honest: it is completely insane that we’re here. That consciousness somehow emerged from stardust and now we’re walking around making art and falling in love and arguing about pineapple on pizza and crying at commercials. The sheer improbability of it should blow our minds daily.

I don’t know what the future holds. Nobody does, despite what certain podcasters and politicians would have you believe. But I know this: whatever comes, I want to meet it with curiosity instead of fear. With wonder instead of cynicism. With the same wide-eyed seven-year-old who thought he could see stars die if he stared hard enough.

Because maybe that’s the secret. Maybe we don’t need to see the stars die. Maybe we just need to keep looking up. Keep noticing. Keep being stupidly, stubbornly, beautifully amazed by the fact that we get to be here at all.

The wind just picked up outside. The trees are doing their dance again. And for the first time in a long time, I feel completely at peace with not knowing what happens next.

(Word count: 1,012)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
